policies.name: Accessibility State Managed
policies.description: Interactive components with ARIA state (expanded, selected, checked, disabled) must manage state transitions correctly. Focus management after state changes should be verified.

policies.name: Accomplishment Summary Produced
policies.description: A Software Accomplishment Summary (SAS) is produced, showing compliance with the PSAC.

policies.name: Acts in Client Best Interest
policies.description: Every AI-supported recommendation is assessed against the overarching MiFID II obligation to act in the client's best interest.

policies.name: Agent Identity Resolved
policies.description: Every agentic action must carry a resolvable AgentID bound to a valid KYA credential (NIST IAL2 human principal).

policies.name: AI Complaints Recorded
policies.description: Firms maintain comprehensive records on AI-related client and potential-client complaints.

policies.name: AI Content Marked
policies.description: AI-generated client-facing content is marked or labelled as AI-generated/synthetic.

policies.name: AI Information Clear, Fair, Not Misleading
policies.description: Information provided to clients on the firm's use of AI is presented in a clear, fair and not misleading manner.

policies.name: AI Information Monitored (ex-post)
policies.description: Ex-post controls monitor and evaluate any process that delivers information to clients directly or indirectly through AI-driven mechanisms.

policies.name: AI Inventory Recorded
policies.description: AI systems producing decisions or outputs are recorded in the firm's AI inventory.

policies.name: AI Outcomes Logged
policies.description: Every AI decision or output is recorded, with the model version that produced it, throughout the AI system lifecycle.

policies.name: AI Output Accuracy Controlled (ex-ante)
policies.description: Ex-ante controls verify the accuracy of information supplied to or by AI before it is disseminated to clients, preventing erroneous or misleading investment advice.

policies.name: AI Records Maintained
policies.description: Records document the use of AI, including the decision-making processes, data sources used, algorithms implemented, and any modifications made over time.

policies.name: AI Stress Tested
policies.description: AI systems are periodically stress-tested to evaluate performance under extreme market conditions.

policies.name: AI Tested Before Deployment
policies.description: AI systems are tested and validated before deployment in the provision of investment services.

policies.name: AI Use Disclosed
policies.description: Clients are informed when an AI system is generating client-facing responses or analysis.

policies.name: AI Use Disclosed to Client
policies.description: Firms transparently disclose to clients when they are interacting with an AI system (e.g. chatbots or other AI-driven automated systems).

policies.name: All Actions Have Rationale
policies.description: Every action in the trace must include a non-empty rationale field explaining why it was taken.

policies.name: All Decisions Justified
policies.description: All gateway (decision) nodes must have a non-empty rationale explaining the choice.

policies.name: Animation State Deterministic
policies.description: CSS/JS animation state machines should be verified to be deterministic — the same sequence of events always produces the same visual state.

policies.name: API Breaking Change Flagged
policies.description: Changes to public API endpoints must be formally checked for backward compatibility. Breaking changes must be explicitly approved.
policies.formula: G(EditFile ∧ api_endpoint → P_chain(Verify ∧ (proved ∧ backward_compatible) ∨ (refuted ∧ P UserApproval)))

policies.name: Approval Before Deploy
policies.description: Deployment actions require explicit user approval.

policies.name: Artifacts Baselined
policies.description: All life-cycle data configuration items are identified and placed under configuration management (baselined).

policies.name: Assumptions Are Located
policies.description: Every residual of kind 'assumption' must be locatable — it must carry a target or a non-empty related_artifact_ids, so a reviewer can find what depends on it.

policies.name: Async State Transitions Verified
policies.description: Async operations (API calls, file uploads) that drive UI state must have their transition logic formally verified — especially error and timeout paths.

policies.name: CERT: Conformance Recorded at Release
policies.description: A conformance record (which rules were checked, findings, deviations) exists before release.

policies.name: CERT: Deviation Documented
policies.description: Every deviation records the violation rationale and is approved.

policies.name: CERT: L1 Findings Remediated
policies.description: Highest-priority (Level L1) findings are remediated.

policies.name: CERT: L2 Findings Reviewed
policies.description: Medium-priority (Level L2) findings are reviewed.

policies.name: CERT: No Open L1 at Release
policies.description: No Level L1 finding remains open at release.

policies.name: CERT: Priority and Level Assigned
policies.description: Each finding has a Priority (Severity × Likelihood × Remediation Cost, 1–27) and resulting Level (L1/L2/L3) assigned.

policies.name: CERT: Recommendation Departure Recorded
policies.description: Departures from CERT Recommendations (advisory guidelines) are recorded.

policies.name: CERT: Risk Assessed
policies.description: Every finding is risk-assessed on the three CERT factors: severity, likelihood, and remediation cost.

policies.name: CERT: Rule Violation Remediated
policies.description: Violations of CERT Rules (normative) are remediated, or covered by an approved deviation.

policies.name: CERT: Static Analysis Before Commit
policies.description: Source is checked against the CERT C / C++ ruleset by static analysis before it is committed.

policies.name: CERT: Undecidable Rule Manually Reviewed
policies.description: Potential violations of undecidable rules (not fully checkable by static analysis) are manually reviewed.

policies.name: Changes Controlled
policies.description: Changes to baselined configuration items occur only through an approved change-control process.

policies.name: CMS: Human Review of AI-Written Policy
policies.description: Use of AI tools to draft CMS policies is followed by human review; AI supports but does not substitute for human decisions.

policies.name: CMS: AI Component Provenance Verified
policies.description: The provenance and integrity of AI components (models, data, code) used in production are verified.

policies.name: CMS: Continuous Human Oversight
policies.description: Continuous human oversight is in place to ensure AI/ML output meets CMS and federal guidelines.

policies.name: CMS: Data Residency / On-Prem Foreign AI
policies.description: Foreign-entity AI tools are used only if deployed on CMS infrastructure with no data sent to the internet (CMS data stays in the U.S.).

policies.name: CMS: External AI Data-Use Agreement
policies.description: External AI tools that can access non-public CMS data have appropriate data-use agreements in place beforehand.

policies.name: CMS: Human Makes High-Impact Final Decision
policies.description: For high-impact cases, AI provides advice/recommendations only; the final decision is made by qualified staff with documented oversight.

policies.name: CMS: High-Impact Use Case Risk-Assessed
policies.description: High-impact AI use cases (per OMB M-25-21) undergo a CMS AI governance risk assessment and apply minimum risk-management practices.

policies.name: CMS: Privacy-Preserving Non-Prod Data
policies.description: Development and lower environments use synthetic or de-identified data rather than real PII/PHI.

policies.name: CMS: Production AI Observability
policies.description: Production AI systems implement observability — traces, evaluations (EVALs), and key metrics tracked over time.

policies.name: CMS: Production Prompts Versioned
policies.description: AI system prompts used in production are versioned and their performance reviewed over time.

policies.name: CMS: AI-Supported Actions Retained
policies.description: AI-supported official actions and AI-generated content are retained per records-retention and FOIA requirements.

policies.name: CMS: Sensitive Data Only With Compliant Tool
policies.description: PHI / sensitive PII and other sensitive data are used only with AI tools that meet HHS/CMS cybersecurity & privacy standards.

policies.name: CMS: Zero-Trust for AI
policies.description: AI tools that can make outbound requests are constrained by data minimization and network segmentation to contain the blast radius.

policies.name: Code Trace to LLR
policies.description: Source code is developed from, and traces to, the low-level requirements.

policies.name: Code Verified
policies.description: Source code is verified (review/analysis) for compliance with the low-level requirements and coding standards.

policies.name: Consent for AI Decisions
policies.description: Client consent is recorded where AI materially affects client rights, account access, or privacy.

policies.name: Consequential Action Approved
policies.description: A consequential action must be preceded by a passed, authenticated approval gate.

policies.name: Counterexample Triggers Fix
policies.description: When a verification goal is refuted (counterexample found), a fix action should follow.

policies.name: Credential Not Expiring
policies.description: KYA/vLEI credentials must not be within the expiry window (proposed threshold < 30 days).

policies.name: Data Flow Integrity
policies.description: Every consumed artifact must have exactly one valid producer earlier in the trace.
policies.formula: G(∀input ∈ inputs(a) → ∃!b . b < a ∧ input ∈ outputs(b))

policies.name: Database Migration Verified
policies.description: Database migration scripts must be formally verified for reversibility and data preservation before execution.

policies.name: DCE Current for Consequential Actions
policies.description: Consequential actions may proceed only while the referenced Deterministic Control Envelope (AlgoCertificate) is current and not invalidated.

policies.name: Decision Logic Recorded
policies.description: Each AI output documents the decision-making logic and reasoning behind it (explainability).

policies.name: Decision Path Reconstructable
policies.description: Each consequential message should carry a DecisionPathID linking to a reconstructable decision graph.

policies.name: Default-Deny Confirmed
policies.description: At Tier 4 a default-deny authorisation must be confirmed before any tool call.

policies.name: Derived Requirements to Safety
policies.description: Derived requirements (not traceable to a higher level) are provided to the system safety assessment process.

policies.name: Destructive Actions Confirmed
policies.description: Delete actions require linked user approval in the dependency chain — not just any prior approval anywhere in the trace.

policies.name: Dual Approval for Critical Autonomous
policies.description: Tier 4 (Critical Autonomous) release requires two distinct authenticated approvals before proceeding.

policies.name: Execution Linked to Intent
policies.description: Every consequential action must resolve to a declared IntentID — the pre-declared objective and constraint set that authorised it.

policies.name: Explain Before Complex Change
policies.description: Complex changes (touching 3+ files) should be preceded by an explanation to the user.

policies.name: Files Modified Consistent
policies.description: The files_modified list must match the actual files changed by code-modifying actions.

policies.name: Form Validation Complete
policies.description: Form validation logic should be formalized and decomposed to ensure all field combinations are handled — no valid input rejected, no invalid input accepted.

policies.name: Formalize Before Verify
policies.description: Verification goals must be preceded by a formalization step that produced the model being verified.

policies.name: Generated Tests Require Decomposition
policies.description: Generated test artifacts must derive from a Decomposition artifact in their lineage.

policies.name: Goals Reference Valid Artifacts
policies.description: Every VerificationGoal must reference a valid IMLModel via its target_artifact_id field.

policies.name: High-Severity Residuals Acknowledged Before Commit
policies.description: Before a commit, every high- or critical-severity residual must be acknowledged, addressed, or waived — not left open. A commit must not silently carry an unacknowledged high-severity gap.
policies.formula: G(GitCommit → ∀ r ∈ residuals . r.severity ≥ High → r.status ≠ Open)

policies.name: HLR Trace to System Requirements
policies.description: High-level requirements are developed from, and trace to, system requirements (bidirectional traceability).

policies.name: HLR Verified
policies.description: High-level requirements are verified for compliance, accuracy and consistency (review/analysis).

policies.name: Human Oversight Evidenced
policies.description: Investor- or market-impacting AI outcomes carry evidence of human oversight or intervention.

policies.name: Incidents Root-Caused
policies.description: AI-related incidents are recorded with root-cause analysis and remediation actions.

policies.name: Certification Cases Generated by Region Decomposition
policies.description: The certification cases a counterparty is tested against must be generated from the IPL model by region decomposition — each case traceable to a symbolic region of the model — rather than hand-written ad hoc.

policies.name: Certification Cases Appropriately Selected
policies.description: Region decomposition produces far more cases than can be executed exhaustively. Certification must run a deliberately selected subset of the generated cases — justified by a documented selection strategy and covering the required behaviour classes and risk-prioritised regions — not every generated case, and not an arbitrary few.

policies.name: Failing Cases Resolved Before Sign-off
policies.description: Certification cannot be signed off while any selected certification case is failing; every conformance failure — whether in the counterparty or in the IPL model — must be resolved and re-run first.

policies.name: Production Drift Surfaced & Resolved
policies.description: In production, live sessions must be checked against the IPL model; any drift between specification and running system must surface as an observable failure and be resolved or raised as an incident.

policies.name: Invalid Flows Exercised
policies.description: Whatever subset of cases is selected, it must include invalid and rejection behaviour, not just happy paths — the IPL model's invalid message flows (validity violations and the expected reject responses) must be among the cases exercised.

policies.name: Certification Mode Declared
policies.description: Each certification run must declare its mode — Simulator (against an IPL-generated simulator) or Proxy (in-line with the live venue) — so its scope and fidelity are on the record.

policies.name: Model Co-simulated Against Live Traffic
policies.description: Before sign-off the IPL model should be co-simulated against captured UAT or production traffic (the digital twin) to confirm the model and the real system agree.

policies.name: IPL Model Valid Before Generation
policies.description: The IPL model must type-check and be free of errors before it is used to generate tests or simulators — only a valid model is guaranteed to produce a functioning implementation.

policies.name: IPL Model & ROE Version Pinned
policies.description: A certification sign-off must pin the exact IPL model version and the ROE version it formalizes, so the result is traceable to a specific formal specification.

policies.name: No Onboarding Without a Passed Certification
policies.description: No counterparty is enabled in production without a prior IPL-based certification that passed and was signed off.

policies.name: Certification Report Produced
policies.description: A completed certification run must produce a certification report tying each verdict back to the IPL model's regions; a run that finishes with no report is not an auditable certification.

policies.name: ROE Formalized in IPL
policies.description: The venue's Rules of Engagement / FIX specification a counterparty is certified against must be formalized as an IPL model — an executable formal spec — before any certification run, not left as prose.

policies.name: JSF++: Certified Libraries Only
policies.description: Only DO-178B Level A certifiable (SEAL 1) C/C++ libraries are used with safety-critical code.

policies.name: JSF++: Deviation Record Maintained
policies.description: Every deviation record documents the rationale and the required approval(s).

policies.name: JSF++: No Dead Code
policies.description: Dead code — executable code or data not traceable to a system or software requirement — is not present.

policies.name: JSF++: Run-Time Checking Provided
policies.description: Safety-critical (SEAL 1/2) software makes provision for run-time checking (defensive programming).

policies.name: JSF++: Shall Deviation Documented In File
policies.description: Each deviation from a 'shall' rule is documented in the file that contains the deviation.

policies.name: JSF++: Shall Rules Verified
policies.description: Compliance with 'shall' rules is verified (automatically or manually) before code is committed.

policies.name: JSF++: Shall Violation Has Deviation
policies.description: Any violation of a 'shall' rule is covered by an approved deviation.

policies.name: JSF++: Shall/Will Deviation Dual-Approved
policies.description: Breaking a 'shall' or 'will' (mandatory) rule is approved by BOTH the software engineering lead and the software product manager.

policies.name: JSF++: Should-Rule Deviation Approved
policies.description: Breaking a 'should' (advisory) rule is approved by the software engineering lead.

policies.name: JSF++: Should Violation Recorded
policies.description: Departures from 'should' (advisory) rules are recorded.

policies.name: JSF++: Static Analysis Before Commit
policies.description: Source is checked against the JSF++ ruleset by static analysis before it is committed.

policies.name: JSF++: Will Violation Has Deviation
policies.description: Any violation of a 'will' rule is covered by an approved deviation.

policies.name: Legal Entity (vLEI) Present
policies.description: Every action must carry the verifiable LEI (vLEI / AgentOwnerLEI) of the regulated entity on whose behalf the agent acts.

policies.name: Lint Before Commit
policies.description: A linter should run before any commit to catch style and quality issues.

policies.name: LLR Trace to HLR
policies.description: Low-level requirements (software design) are developed from, and trace to, high-level requirements.

policies.name: LLR Verified
policies.description: Low-level requirements / design are verified by review or analysis.

policies.name: MISRA C: Advisory Violation Recorded
policies.description: Violations of Advisory guidelines are recorded (a formal deviation is not required, but the decision is documented).

policies.name: MISRA C: Compliance Summary Produced
policies.description: A Guidelines Compliance Summary (GCS) is produced as the primary record of overall project compliance.

policies.name: MISRA C: Deviation Documented
policies.description: Every deviation records the violation and its supporting rationale, and is approved through the deviation procedure.

policies.name: MISRA C: Directives Addressed With Evidence
policies.description: Directives (process/design guidelines, whose compliance depends on project context) are addressed with documented evidence.

policies.name: MISRA C: Enforcement Plan Defined
policies.description: Each guideline analysed has a defined enforcement means in the Guidelines Enforcement Plan.

policies.name: MISRA C: Mandatory Not Weakened
policies.description: A Mandatory guideline is never re-categorized to a less strict category; only Advisory guidelines may be re-categorized to Disapplied.

policies.name: MISRA C: No Deviation Against Mandatory
policies.description: No deviation is raised against a Mandatory guideline.

policies.name: MISRA C: No Mandatory Violation
policies.description: Mandatory guidelines are never violated — no deviation is permitted against a Mandatory guideline.

policies.name: MISRA C: Re-categorization in GRP
policies.description: Any change to a guideline's category is documented in the Guidelines Re-categorization Plan (GRP).

policies.name: MISRA C: Required Violation Has Deviation
policies.description: Any violation of a Required guideline is covered by an approved deviation.

policies.name: MISRA C: Static Analysis Before Commit
policies.description: Source is checked against the MISRA C:2012 ruleset by static analysis before it is committed.

policies.name: MISRA C: Undecidable Guideline Manually Reviewed
policies.description: Potential violations of undecidable guidelines (not fully checkable by static analysis) are subject to manual review.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Advisory Violation Recorded
policies.description: Violations of Advisory guidelines are recorded (a formal deviation is not required, but the decision is documented).

policies.name: MISRA C++: Compliance Summary Produced
policies.description: A Guidelines Compliance Summary (GCS) is produced as the primary record of overall project compliance.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Deviation Documented
policies.description: Every deviation records the violation and its supporting rationale, and is approved through the deviation procedure.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Directives Addressed With Evidence
policies.description: Directives (process/design guidelines, whose compliance depends on project context) are addressed with documented evidence.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Enforcement Plan Defined
policies.description: Each guideline analysed has a defined enforcement means in the Guidelines Enforcement Plan.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Mandatory Not Weakened
policies.description: A Mandatory guideline is never re-categorized to a less strict category; only Advisory guidelines may be re-categorized to Disapplied.

policies.name: MISRA C++: No Deviation Against Mandatory
policies.description: No deviation is raised against a Mandatory guideline.

policies.name: MISRA C++: No Mandatory Violation
policies.description: Mandatory guidelines are never violated — no deviation is permitted against a Mandatory guideline.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Re-categorization in GRP
policies.description: Any change to a guideline's category is documented in the Guidelines Re-categorization Plan (GRP).

policies.name: MISRA C++: Required Violation Has Deviation
policies.description: Any violation of a Required guideline is covered by an approved deviation.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Static Analysis Before Commit
policies.description: Source is checked against the MISRA C++:2023 ruleset by static analysis before it is committed.

policies.name: MISRA C++: Undecidable Guideline Manually Reviewed
policies.description: Potential violations of undecidable guidelines (not fully checkable by static analysis) are subject to manual review.

policies.name: No AI-Washing
policies.description: Public claims about the firm's AI are substantiated and not overstated (no 'AI-washing').

policies.name: No Force Push
policies.description: Force push to shared branches is prohibited. Agents must never rewrite published history.

policies.name: No Guard Violation
policies.description: No runtime guard (alignment/confidence/policy-boundary) violation may occur in the trajectory.

policies.name: No Open Critical Residuals
policies.description: A trace must carry no critical-severity residual that is still open. Intended as an approval gate: critical declared gaps must be addressed, acknowledged, or waived before sign-off.
policies.formula: ¬∃ r ∈ residuals . r.severity = Critical ∧ r.status = Open

policies.name: No Prohibited Transition
policies.description: No action may attempt a transition that is not defined in the governed transition system for the current state.

policies.name: No Release Without Authenticated Approval
policies.description: External-facing release or dispatch may occur only after an authenticated approval event whose scope covers the intended action (Szpruch capability C4: Gated Release & Dispatch).

policies.name: No Secrets Committed
policies.description: Files matching secret patterns (.env, credentials, private keys) must never be included in commits.

policies.name: Numeric Recomputed Deterministically (C2)
policies.description: Capability C2 (Deterministic Numeric Computation): numeric results must be recomputed by a deterministic (non-LLM) calculator, not asserted by the model.

policies.name: Output-to-Action Traceable
policies.description: Every consequential action is traceable to a logged AI output that preceded it.

policies.name: Outputs Policy-Constrained (C3)
policies.description: Capability C3 (Policy-Constrained Drafting): drafted outputs must comply with approved templates and policy rules.

policies.name: Outsourced AI Due Diligence
policies.description: Third-party AI used for critical or important operational functions is subject to MiFID II outsourcing due diligence and adequate controls.

policies.name: PCI Compliance Review
policies.description: Changes to payment card processing code must be preceded by formal reasoning that verifies PCI DSS compliance constraints — no card data in logs, encryption at rest, tokenization of PANs.

policies.name: Plaid Token Lifecycle Conformance
policies.description: Code handling Plaid Link tokens must be verified against the Plaid Link Reference Model — token exchange only after successful link, proper expiration handling.

policies.name: Plans Before Development
policies.description: Software plans (PSAC, SDP, SVP, SCMP, SQAP) are established and approved before development of life-cycle data begins.

policies.name: Policy Reference Current
policies.description: The governing PolicyRefID must reference a current, certified policy document (not superseded or revoked).

policies.name: Problem Reports Closed
policies.description: Problem reports are tracked to closure — resolved, or deferred with recorded rationale.

policies.name: Product Governance Aligned
policies.description: AI-driven distribution aligns the products distributed to their identified target market (product governance).

policies.name: PSAC Agreed With Authority
policies.description: The Plan for Software Aspects of Certification (PSAC) is agreed with the certification authority before development proceeds.

policies.name: React Effect Cleanup Verified
policies.description: React useEffect hooks with subscriptions or timers should have their cleanup logic formally verified to prevent memory leaks.

policies.name: Reasoning Required for High-Stakes Changes
policies.description: Edits to high-stakes modules (payments, risk, matching) must be preceded by formal reasoning in the artifact dependency chain.

policies.name: Recommendation Suitable
policies.description: AI-supported recommendations are subject to a suitability assessment aligning them to the client's situation, objectives, sustainability preferences, risk tolerance, and knowledge.

policies.name: Reducer Action Coverage
policies.description: Redux/useReducer reducers should be decomposed to verify that every action type is handled and produces a valid state.

policies.name: Regulated Change Audit Trail
policies.description: Changes to regulated systems must have a complete audit trail: research action, formal reasoning, user approval, test pass, and commit — all with rationale.
policies.formula: G(GitCommit ∧ regulated_scope → P_chain(ReadFile) ∧ P_chain(Verify ∧ proved) ∧ P(UserApproval) ∧ P(RunTests ∧ completed))

policies.name: Reportable Incident Notified
policies.description: Reportable AI incidents are notified to the supervisory authority.

policies.name: Research Before Edit
policies.description: Every edit must be preceded by at least one research action (read, search, analyze) on the same target file.

policies.name: Retrieved Data Attributed (C1)
policies.description: Capability C1 (Retrieval & Attribution): retrieved data must carry provenance and recency checks before it is relied upon.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: GOVERN — Accountability Assigned
policies.description: Clear roles, responsibilities and lines of accountability for the AI system are established.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: GOVERN — Legal Requirements Mapped
policies.description: Applicable legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood and managed.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: GOVERN — Risk Policies In Place
policies.description: AI risk management policies and processes are in place and applied across the AI lifecycle.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MANAGE — Incident Response
policies.description: AI incidents and negative impacts are responded to, recovered from, and communicated.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MANAGE — Risk Treated
policies.description: Each identified risk is treated — mitigated, transferred, avoided, or formally accepted.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MANAGE — Risks Prioritized
policies.description: AI risks are prioritized and acted upon based on assessment and impact.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MANAGE — Third-Party Risks
policies.description: Risks from third-party AI resources (models, data, services) are identified and managed.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MAP — Context Established
policies.description: The context — intended purpose, setting, and the AI system's role — is established and documented before deployment.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MAP — Impacts Characterized
policies.description: Potential impacts, benefits and risks to individuals, groups, communities and society are mapped.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MAP — System Categorized
policies.description: The AI system is categorized by its capabilities, intended use, and risk level.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MEASURE — Fairness & Bias
policies.description: The AI system is measured for fairness, with harmful bias identified and managed.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MEASURE — Ongoing Tracking
policies.description: Mechanisms are in place to track identified AI risks and system performance over time.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MEASURE — Security & Privacy
policies.description: The AI system is measured for the secure-&-resilient and privacy-enhanced characteristics.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MEASURE — Transparency & Explainability
policies.description: The AI system is measured for the accountable-&-transparent and explainable-&-interpretable characteristics.

policies.name: NIST AI RMF: MEASURE — Validity & Safety
policies.description: The AI system is measured for the validity & reliability and safety trustworthiness characteristics before deployment.

policies.name: Route Guard Complete
policies.description: Route guards (auth checks, role checks, feature flags) must be decomposed to verify that all route × auth-state combinations are handled correctly.

policies.name: SQA Conformity Review
policies.description: Software Quality Assurance assures that life-cycle processes and outputs comply with the approved plans and standards (conformity review).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PO — Roles & Responsibilities
policies.description: Roles and responsibilities for the secure development process are defined (PO.2).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PO — Security Requirements Defined
policies.description: Security requirements for the software and its development are defined (PO.1).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PO — Toolchain Secured
policies.description: Supporting toolchains and development environments are configured to improve security (PO.3).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PS — Provenance / SBOM Recorded
policies.description: Provenance data (e.g. an SBOM) for each release is recorded (PS.3).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PS — Release Archived
policies.description: Each release and its associated files are archived and protected (PS.1/PS.3).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PS — Release Integrity Protected
policies.description: Each software release is integrity-protected (e.g. cryptographically signed) against tampering (PS.2).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PW — No Open Vulnerability at Release
policies.description: No known unaddressed vulnerability remains open at release.

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PW — Secure Default Settings
policies.description: Software is configured with secure default settings (PW.9).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PW — Security Review / Analysis
policies.description: Code is reviewed and/or analyzed for security before it is accepted (PW.7/PW.8).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PW — Security Tested
policies.description: Executable code is security-tested before release (PW.8).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: PW — Threat Modeled
policies.description: The design is reviewed against security requirements and threats (threat modeling) (PW.1/PW.2).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: RV — Root-Cause Analysis
policies.description: Root-cause analysis is performed to reduce the frequency of future vulnerabilities (RV.3).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: RV — Vulnerability Analysis
policies.description: Releases are continuously analyzed to identify vulnerabilities (RV.1).

policies.name: NIST SSDF: RV — Vulnerability Remediated
policies.description: Each identified vulnerability is assessed and remediated or mitigated (RV.2).

policies.name: Staff Competent for AI
policies.description: Staff using AI to provide information about investment products to clients do so under heightened vigilance and with adequate AI competence and training.

policies.name: Development Standards Defined
policies.description: Software requirements, design, and coding standards are defined before the corresponding life-cycle data is produced.

policies.name: Stripe Conformance Required
policies.description: Formalizations of Stripe payment code must be verified against the Stripe Payment Reference Model.

policies.name: Structural Coverage Achieved
policies.description: Structural coverage analysis confirms the achieved coverage appropriate to the DAL (MC/DC at A, decision at B, statement at C).

policies.name: Telemetry Spans Complete
policies.description: Every required governance-semantic telemetry span (capability invocations, tool calls, guard evaluations, approval events) must be recorded.

policies.name: Test Traces to Requirement
policies.description: Every test case traces to the requirement it verifies (bidirectional requirements-to-test traceability).

policies.name: Tests Before Commit
policies.description: Every commit must be preceded by a successful test run.

policies.name: Tests Pass Before Commit
policies.description: The most recent test run before each commit must have passed. A failed run followed by a successful re-run satisfies this policy.

policies.name: Requirements-Based Testing
policies.description: Test cases are requirements-based, exercising the software against its requirements (normal and robustness).

policies.name: Tool-Call Budget
policies.description: The number of tool calls in a single workflow must stay within the per-workflow budget. Budget shown as 20; set per deployment.

policies.name: Tool Calls Allowlisted
policies.description: Every tool/capability invocation must lie within the certified allowlist for the agent's tier.

policies.name: Trace Has Proper Lifecycle
policies.description: The trace must have both a start event (trigger) and eventually reach an end event (outcome).

policies.name: Training Data Representative
policies.description: Data used as input for AI systems is relevant, sufficient and representative, and algorithms are trained and validated on accurate, comprehensive and sufficiently broad datasets.

policies.name: Trajectory Within Bound (Lmax)
policies.description: The execution trajectory length must stay within the certified maximum (Lmax). Bound shown as 50; set per deployment.

policies.name: Two-Person Approval for Infrastructure
policies.description: Infrastructure changes (database migrations, IAM policies, network configs) require approval from two distinct users before execution.
policies.formula: G(EditFile ∧ infra_scope → P(UserApproval ∧ approver_1) ∧ P(UserApproval ∧ approver_2 ∧ approver_2 ≠ approver_1))

policies.name: Type Check Before Commit
policies.description: A type checker should run before any commit for typed languages.

policies.name: UI State Machine Decomposed
policies.description: Complex UI state machines (5+ states) should be decomposed into regions to generate exhaustive edge case tests.

policies.name: Unverified Residuals Have a Suggested Check
policies.description: Every residual of kind 'unverified' must carry a non-empty suggested_check — a concrete way a reviewer could close the gap.

policies.name: Verification Independence
policies.description: At DAL A/B, verification of the development outputs is performed with independence.

policies.name: Within Constraint Scope
policies.description: Every action must fall within the active ConstraintSet binding the execution (limits, permissions, risk envelope).

policies.name: XState Deadlock Free
policies.description: XState machines must be verified to be deadlock-free — every non-final state must have at least one enabled transition.

policies.name: XState Guards Verified
policies.description: Guard conditions in XState machines must be formally verified to ensure they partition the event space completely — no unhandled cases.

policies.name: XState Machine Formalized
policies.description: Any XState state machine definition must be formalized into IML before the component using it is edited.

policies.name: XState Reachability Checked
policies.description: All states in an XState machine should be reachable from the initial state. Unreachable states indicate dead code or design errors.

general.label: General
general.description: Universal agent workflow and safety policies

formal-reasoning.label: Formal Reasoning
formal-reasoning.description: Policies governing the formal verification pipeline

payments.label: Payments
payments.description: Payment processing and financial API conformance

xstate.label: XState
xstate.description: XState state machine formalization and verification

react.label: React
react.description: React component lifecycle and hook verification

javascript-ui.label: JavaScript UI
javascript-ui.description: Frontend UI logic, routing, forms, animations, accessibility

agentic-governance.label: Agentic AI Governance
agentic-governance.description: Runtime governance for agentic AI workflows — the FIX AI WG traffic-light scheme (identity, intent, capability, telemetry, approval) as computable policies.

ai-supervision.label: AI Supervision
ai-supervision.description: Supervisory recordkeeping, audit-trail and disclosure policies for AI in capital markets — the IOSCO toolkit's evidence requirements as machine-checkable rules.

investment-services.label: Investment Services
investment-services.description: MiFID II conduct policies for AI in retail investment services — ESMA's best-interest, suitability, accuracy, and recordkeeping expectations as machine-checkable rules.

software-assurance.label: Software Assurance
software-assurance.description: Airborne software certification objectives (RTCA DO-178C) — traceability, verification, coverage and configuration management as machine-checkable rules over the software life-cycle record.

coding-standards.label: Coding Standards
coding-standards.description: MISRA C/C++ compliance-process policies — enforcement, deviations, re-categorization and compliance summary as machine-checkable rules over a coding/CI trace.

ai-risk-management.label: AI Risk Management
ai-risk-management.description: Lifecycle AI risk-management policies — the NIST AI RMF's Govern/Map/Measure/Manage functions and trustworthiness characteristics as machine-checkable rules.

secure-development.label: Secure Development
secure-development.description: Secure software development lifecycle practices — the NIST SSDF's Prepare/Protect/Produce/Respond groups, including supply-chain integrity and vulnerability response, as machine-checkable rules.

fix-certification.label: FIX Certification
fix-certification.description: Best practice for certifying a counterparty against a venue's FIX specification using IPL (Imandra Protocol Language) — formalize the Rules of Engagement as an IPL model, generate the conformance suite by region decomposition, certify in Simulator or Proxy mode, and monitor for drift — as machine-checkable rules over the certification trace.

organizations.name: CERT (SEI)
organizations.full_name: SEI CERT Secure Coding Standards (Carnegie Mellon Software Engineering Institute)
organizations.description: The SEI CERT Coding Standards (CERT C and CERT C++) are secure-coding standards from Carnegie Mellon's Software Engineering Institute. Unlike category-based standards, CERT scores every guideline on a risk model — severity, likelihood, and remediation cost — yielding a priority and level (L1/L2/L3) that drive remediation order. That risk model is the basis for the pack below.
mapping: CERT's individual rules and recommendations are checked by static analysis — so ponens does not re-encode them. What is distinctive about CERT, and what ponens governs, is its risk-prioritized remediation process. Every finding is scored on three factors (severity × likelihood × remediation cost), which multiply to a Priority (1–27) and a Level (L1 = 12–27, L2 = 6–9, L3 = 1–4). ponens turns that into policies over a coding/CI trace: every finding is risk-assessed and assigned a priority and level, L1 findings are remediated and never shipped open, and L2 findings are reviewed.

CERT also distinguishes normative Rules (a violation is a defect that must be fixed or formally deviated) from advisory Recommendations (departures recorded), and — like MISRA and JSF — requires analysis before commit and manual review of undecidable rules. The same methodology covers both CERT C and CERT C++, so this is a single pack rather than two. Running it with `ponens trace check` aggregates to Green / Amber / Red: an open L1 at release, an unremediated rule violation, a missing risk assessment, or no conformance record is Red; recommendation departures, L2 review, and undecidable-rule review are Amber.

organizations.name: CMS
organizations.full_name: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (Technical Reference Architecture)
organizations.description: CMS — the US agency for Medicare & Medicaid — publishes AI Guidance in its Technical Reference Architecture: concrete business rules (BR-AI-1..6) and operational practices for using AI responsibly with sensitive healthcare data. Where the NIST AI RMF is the generic risk lifecycle, CMS is the operational enforcement layer, and it is the basis for the pack below.
mapping: CMS AI Guidance is an operational, enforceable layer on top of the federal frameworks (it references OMB M-25-21/M-25-22 and the NIST AI RMF). Its six business rules and recommended practices map directly to ponens policies over an AI system's operation record: high-impact use cases (the OMB M-25-21 definition) get a risk assessment and a human final decision; sensitive data (PHI/SPII) is used only with approved tools; foreign-entity AI may run only on CMS infrastructure with no internet egress; AI-supported official actions are retained; and continuous human oversight is required.

It also specifies concrete operational security that distinguishes it from the lifecycle frameworks: verify the provenance and integrity of AI components (System Composition Analysis), apply zero-trust — data minimization and network segmentation — to AI tools that can reach outbound, and instrument production AI with observability. Notably, CMS mandates tracking 'traces, EVALs, prompt management/versioning, and key metrics' — which is precisely the governance-semantic telemetry ponens evaluates, so this pack is close to a literal implementation of the guidance. Running it with `ponens trace check` aggregates to Green / Amber / Red across use-case governance, data protection & residency, supply-chain/zero-trust, and observability/records.

organizations.name: ESMA
organizations.description: ESMA is the EU's securities-markets regulator. Its Public Statement on the use of AI in the provision of retail investment services maps AI use onto firms' existing MiFID II obligations — best interest, suitability, transparency, risk management, and recordkeeping — the basis for the pack below.
mapping: ESMA's Public Statement does not create new AI rules; it reads AI use through existing MiFID II obligations, with one throughline — “firms' decisions remain the responsibility of management bodies, irrespective of whether those decisions are taken by people or AI-based tools,” and AI must always serve clients' best interest. ponens turns each of those obligations into a policy over the trace of an AI-assisted investment service: that the client is told when AI is involved, that recommendations are assessed for best interest and suitability, that AI outputs are accuracy-checked before reaching clients and monitored afterwards, and that models are tested on representative data before deployment.

The Statement's recordkeeping clause (¶24) and its ex-ante / ex-post accuracy controls (¶16) read almost directly as trace policies, so the mapping is tight rather than interpretive. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-obligation verdicts to a Green / Amber / Red picture: an `error`-severity breach (e.g. an undisclosed AI interaction, an unsuitable recommendation, or missing AI records) is Red; softer expectations such as periodic stress-testing or staff competence are Amber. The firm-level MiFID II machinery the Statement also invokes — management-body oversight and outsourcing governance — is organisational rather than per-trace and sits outside this pack.

organizations.name: FIX Community
organizations.full_name: FIX Trading Community — AI Working Group
organizations.description: The FIX Trading Community standardises electronic trading. Its AI Working Group is developing runtime-governance standards for agentic AI in capital markets — the basis for the policy pack below.
mapping: The FIX AI Working Group's runtime-governance proposal defines a traffic-light scheme — Green (proceed), Amber (flag/refer), Red (halt) — across five domains: identity & authorisation, intent & constraint, capability & DCE, runtime telemetry & trajectory, and approval & release gating. ponens maps each traffic-light condition onto a temporal-logic policy evaluated over an agent's execution trace. This is exactly the form the proposal itself demands of any binding control (§10.3): “a deterministic function over the governed state… executing independently of the language model.” A ponens policy is precisely that function.

Each domain becomes a policy group, plus the Szpruch et al. capability failure modes (C1–C4); a policy's severity encodes Red (error) versus Amber (warning), and its tier tag selects the Assistive–to–Critical-Autonomous profile. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-policy verdicts into the GovernanceState the proposal wants carried in a new FIX field — all-pass is Green, a warning-fail is Amber, an error-fail is Red. The genuinely population-level conditions (orchestration drift, compound-workflow market-disorder testing) are the proposal's own Gap 5 and sit outside per-trace policy.

organizations.name: Imandra
organizations.description: Imandra builds automated reasoning for safety-critical systems. Its Imandra Protocol Language (IPL) formalizes a trading venue's Rules of Engagement as a precise, executable model — from which Imandra Connectivity derives conformance test suites, on-demand simulators, certification reports and drift monitoring. This pack captures the best practice for certifying counterparties against a FIX specification using IPL.
mapping: IPL compresses a venue's near-infinite FIX state space into a finite set of symbolic regions via the ImandraX reasoner's Region Decomposition; each region becomes one certification case the counterparty must pass. Where IPL establishes *whether* a counterparty conforms, ponens governs *that the certification process was actually followed* — the Rules of Engagement were formalized and version-pinned as an IPL model, the certification cases were generated from the model's regions (not hand-written), an appropriate subset was selected and justified (with invalid and rejection flows kept in scope), every failing case was resolved, and no counterparty reached production without a passed, reported certification.

Each stage of the Imandra Connectivity workflow — formalize the ROE, generate tests, certify (Simulator or Proxy), co-simulate against live traffic, and monitor for drift — becomes a policy group evaluated by `ponens trace check` over the certification trace. A divergence between the IPL specification and a live session is the observable failure the digital twin is designed to surface, raised here as an error-severity policy.

organizations.name: IOSCO
organizations.description: IOSCO is the global standard-setter for securities markets. Its FR/02/2026 Supervisory Toolkit for AI Use in Capital Markets sets out the records, audit trail, and disclosures supervisors expect firms to be able to evidence — the basis for the pack below.
mapping: The IOSCO Supervisory Toolkit is supervisor-facing: for each area it lists the “Supporting Evidence for Review” a firm must be able to produce. ponens treats that evidence column as the specification. Each recordkeeping and disclosure expectation from Tables 5 and 6 becomes a policy over the AI system's decision/output trace — that decisions and outputs are logged and model-version-stamped, traceable to the actions they drive, explainable, human-overseen where material, incident-recorded with root cause, and properly disclosed to clients. ponens makes that evidence computable rather than a manual document review.

Where the FIX pack is preventive — gating execution in real time — this pack is evidentiary: it computes the audit trail a supervisor would otherwise inspect by hand. `ponens trace check` returns pass / warning / error per requirement and aggregates to a Green / Amber / Red picture of exactly which records or disclosures are missing. The toolkit's organisational layers — monitoring indicators, third-party concentration, and firm-level governance such as board oversight and training — are population- or firm-level rather than per-trace, and so are intentionally out of scope for this pack.

organizations.name: JSF (Lockheed Martin)
organizations.full_name: JSF Air Vehicle C++ Coding Standards — F-35 program (Lockheed Martin)
organizations.description: The JSF Air Vehicle C++ Coding Standards ("JSF++") were developed for the Lockheed Martin F-35 Joint Strike Fighter, with input from Bjarne Stroustrup. Like MISRA they are a safety-critical C++ standard, but with a distinctive three-tier rule scheme (Shall / Will / Should) and a tiered deviation-approval process — the basis for the pack below.
mapping: JSF++ is a C++ coding standard whose 221 numbered rules are checked by static analysis — so, as with MISRA, ponens does not re-encode the rules. What it governs is JSF++'s distinctive compliance process: its three rule categories (Shall = mandatory and verified, Will = mandatory without verification, Should = advisory) and its tiered deviation-approval chain (AV Rules 4-7). Each becomes a policy over a coding/CI trace — breaking a 'should' needs engineering-lead approval, breaking a 'shall' or 'will' needs the engineering lead AND the product manager, and every 'shall' deviation must be documented in the file that contains it.

The standard also carries explicit safety-critical obligations that map cleanly to per-trace policies: provision for run-time checking (AV Rule 15), use of only DO-178B Level A certifiable libraries for safety-critical code (AV Rule 16 — a direct link to the DO-178C pack), and the prohibition on dead code not traceable to a requirement. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates to Green / Amber / Red: an undeviated shall/will violation, a single-approved shall deviation, uncertified safety-critical libraries, or dead code is Red; should-rule deviations and records are Amber.

organizations.name: MISRA
organizations.description: MISRA publishes the most widely used C and C++ coding guidelines for safety- and security-critical software. Its Compliance:2020 document defines how a project demonstrates compliance — enforcement, deviations, re-categorization, and a compliance summary — which is the basis for the packs below.
mapping: MISRA's individual guidelines (143+ rules and directives) are checked by static analysis of source code, not by a trace — so ponens does not re-encode the rules. What ponens governs is the MISRA Compliance:2020 *process*: the four required artifacts (Guidelines Enforcement Plan, Re-categorization Plan, Compliance Summary, and deviation records) and the category rules (Mandatory / Required / Advisory). Each becomes a policy over a coding/CI trace — that static analysis runs against the ruleset before commit, that Mandatory guidelines are never deviated, that every Required violation carries an approved deviation with rationale, that re-categorizations are recorded in the GRP, and that a Compliance Summary is produced.

This is the natural division of labour for an AI coding agent: the analyzer finds the rule violations, and ponens checks that the agent followed the compliance process around them — making the analyzer's output auditable rather than advisory. The two packs share this process; they differ only in the ruleset they enforce (`misra_c_ruleset` for MISRA C:2012, `misra_cpp_ruleset` for MISRA C++:2023) and the guidelines they reference. Running a pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates to Green / Amber / Red: a Mandatory violation, an undeviated Required violation, or a missing Compliance Summary is Red; advisory records, enforcement-plan coverage, and undecidable-rule review are Amber.

organizations.name: NIST
organizations.full_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
organizations.description: NIST publishes the foundational AI and software-security frameworks used worldwide. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) manages AI risk across the lifecycle (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage), and its Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF, SP 800-218) defines secure-development practices (Prepare / Protect / Produce / Respond). Both are voluntary, and both are the basis for the packs below.
mapping: The NIST AI RMF is a lifecycle risk-management framework, not a runtime or conduct regime, so it brings a shape distinct from the financial-regulator packs: four functions — GOVERN (policies, accountability, culture), MAP (context, categorization, impacts), MEASURE (evaluate the seven trustworthiness characteristics), and MANAGE (prioritize, treat, respond, third-party). ponens turns each function's subcategories into policies over an AI system's lifecycle record: that risk policies and accountable roles exist, that context and impacts were mapped and the system categorized before deployment, that the trustworthiness characteristics were measured, and that risks are treated and incidents responded to.

The seven trustworthiness characteristics — valid & reliable, safe, secure & resilient, accountable & transparent, explainable & interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair (harmful bias managed) — land under MEASURE as the conditions a system must be evaluated against before deployment. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates to Green / Amber / Red: missing risk policies, no impact mapping, unmeasured safety/fairness, or an unhandled incident is Red; legal-requirement mapping, transparency/explainability measurement, ongoing tracking and third-party risk are Amber. The framework is voluntary, so this pack is a way to make AI-RMF adoption auditable rather than aspirational.

organizations.name: RTCA
organizations.description: RTCA (with EUROCAE) publishes DO-178C, the airborne-software certification standard used worldwide by aviation authorities. Its Annex A objectives — traceability, verification, coverage, configuration management, and certification liaison — are the basis for the pack below.
mapping: DO-178C assures airborne software by objectives, organised in Annex A tables A-1 to A-10 and scaled by Design Assurance Level (A–E). Its defining requirement is bidirectional traceability — system requirements → high-level requirements → low-level requirements → source code → tests — and ponens expresses that directly with its `P_chain` lineage operator over the software life-cycle record treated as a trace. Each traceability objective becomes a policy such as `G(SourceCode → P_chain(LLR))`, while process-ordering objectives (plans before development, PSAC agreed before development, reviews and structural coverage achieved) become temporal policies over the same trace.

Design Assurance Level maps to a policy's `tier` (A–D) and the 'with independence' objectives map to Amber escalations, so the pack scales the way DO-178C does. Running it with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-objective verdicts to a Green / Amber / Red picture: a missing traceability link, an unverified requirement, an unbaselined configuration item, or an absent Accomplishment Summary is a Red certification gap; softer items such as problem-report closure or independence are Amber. The process-organisation objectives that are not evidenced per artifact (e.g. tool qualification under DO-330) sit outside this pack.

packs.name: Agentic AI Runtime Governance
packs.summary: The FIX AI Working Group's traffic-light (Green / Amber / Red) runtime-governance scheme, expressed as computable policies over an agent's execution trace.
packs.description: Maps the FIX AI WG proposal on Agentic AI Runtime Governance onto ponens policies. Each traffic-light condition across five domains — identity & authorisation, intent & constraint, capability & DCE, runtime telemetry & trajectory, and approval & release gating — plus the Szpruch et al. capability failure modes (C1–C4), becomes a deterministic check that `ponens trace check` aggregates into a Green/Amber/Red GovernanceState.
packs.source: FIX AI WG — Proposal on Agentic AI Runtime Governance (Healey & Houston, Jun 2026); Szpruch, Sudjianto, Bhatti & Ang (2026).

packs.name: CERT C/C++ Secure Coding
packs.summary: The SEI CERT secure-coding risk model — Severity × Likelihood × Remediation → Priority → Level L1/L2/L3 — and its prioritized-remediation process, as computable policies. Covers CERT C and CERT C++.
packs.description: Maps the SEI CERT Coding Standards' distinctive risk-assessment and prioritized-remediation process onto ponens policies. The individual rules are enforced by static analysis; this pack checks that every finding is risk-assessed (severity/likelihood/remediation), assigned a priority and level, that L1 findings are remediated and never shipped, and that Rules vs Recommendations are handled accordingly. Applies to both CERT C and CERT C++.
packs.source: SEI CERT C and CERT C++ Secure Coding Standards (Carnegie Mellon SEI).

packs.name: CMS AI Guidance (TRA)
packs.summary: The CMS Technical Reference Architecture AI Guidance — business rules BR-AI-1..6 plus operational practices (provenance, zero-trust, observability) — as computable policies over an AI system's operation record.
packs.description: Maps the CMS TRA AI Guidance onto ponens policies: high-impact use-case governance and human oversight (BR-AI-2/4/5), sensitive-data and data-residency rules (BR-AI-1/3), AI supply-chain provenance and zero-trust security, and observability/prompt-versioning/records-retention (BR-AI-6). The operational enforcement layer above the NIST AI RMF, for federal healthcare AI.

packs.name: DO-178C Software Assurance
packs.summary: DO-178C's Annex A objectives — traceability, verification, structural coverage, configuration management, and certification liaison — as computable policies over the software life-cycle record.
packs.description: Maps the RTCA DO-178C / EUROCAE ED-12C airborne-software objectives onto ponens policies. Bidirectional traceability (system → HLR → LLR → code → test) is expressed with ponens' P_chain lineage operator; planning, verification, coverage, configuration-management, quality-assurance and certification-liaison objectives become temporal policies. Design Assurance Level maps to each policy's tier (A–D).
packs.source: RTCA DO-178C / EUROCAE ED-12C — Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification.

packs.name: MiFID II AI in Investment Services
packs.summary: ESMA's MiFID II expectations for AI in retail investment services — best interest, suitability, transparency, accuracy controls, testing, and recordkeeping — as computable policies.
packs.description: Maps the ESMA Public Statement on AI in the provision of retail investment services onto ponens policies. Each MiFID II obligation ESMA highlights — acting in the client's best interest, disclosing AI use, suitability and product governance, ex-ante/ex-post accuracy controls, model testing on representative data, outsourcing due diligence, staff competence, and recordkeeping of AI decision processes — becomes a deterministic check over the trace of an AI-assisted investment service.

packs.name: Supervisory Recordkeeping & Disclosure
packs.summary: The IOSCO Supervisory Toolkit's recordkeeping, audit-trail and disclosure expectations, expressed as computable policies over an AI system's decision/execution trace.
packs.description: Maps Tables 5 (Disclosure) and 6 (Recordkeeping & reporting) of the IOSCO FR/02/2026 Supervisory Toolkit onto ponens policies. Where the FIX pack is preventive runtime governance, this pack is evidentiary: it checks that AI decisions and outputs are logged, traceable to the actions they drive, explainable, human-overseen where material, incident-recorded, and properly disclosed to clients — i.e. it computes the 'Supporting Evidence for Review' a supervisor would otherwise inspect by hand.
packs.source: IOSCO FR/02/2026 — Supervisory Toolkit for AI Use in Capital Markets (May 2026).

packs.name: FIX Certification with IPL
packs.summary: Best practice for certifying a counterparty against a venue's FIX specification using IPL — formalize the Rules of Engagement as an IPL model, generate the certification cases by region decomposition, certify in Simulator or Proxy mode, and monitor for drift — as computable policies over the certification trace.
packs.description: Imandra Connectivity certifies a counterparty's FIX implementation against a venue's Rules of Engagement (ROE) by formalizing the ROE as an Imandra Protocol Language (IPL) model, then deriving everything downstream from it: the certification cases — generated from the IPL model by the ImandraX reasoner's Region Decomposition — plus on-demand simulators, a certification report, and continuous drift monitoring. This pack encodes the best practices of that workflow as deterministic checks over the certification trace — the ROE must be a valid, version-pinned IPL model; the certification cases must be generated from the model's symbolic regions and an appropriately selected, justified subset run (covering invalid flows and risk-prioritised regions — not every case, and not an arbitrary few); certification must run in a declared mode and produce a report; every failing region must be resolved; the model must be co-simulated against live traffic before sign-off; and no counterparty reaches production without a passed certification, after which spec-vs-system drift must surface as an observable failure. `ponens trace check` turns the per-policy verdicts into a single certification gate.
packs.source: Imandra Connectivity / IPL — Imandra Protocol Language for formalizing FIX Rules of Engagement (https://www.imandra.ai/connectivity, https://ipl.dev).

packs.name: JSF Air Vehicle C++
packs.summary: The JSF++ (F-35) C++ compliance process — Shall/Will/Should categories, tiered deviation approval, verification, and safety-critical library/run-time obligations — as computable policies.
packs.description: Maps the JSF Air Vehicle C++ Coding Standards' compliance process onto ponens policies. The 221 coding rules are enforced by static analysis; this pack checks the process around them — the Shall/Will/Should deviation-approval chain (engineering lead for should; lead + product manager for shall/will; shall deviations documented in-file), shall-rule verification, run-time checking, certified libraries, and no dead code.
packs.source: JSF Air Vehicle C++ Coding Standards (Lockheed Martin, Rev C, 2005).

packs.name: MISRA C
packs.summary: The MISRA Compliance:2020 process for MISRA C:2012 — enforcement, deviations, re-categorization, and compliance summary — as computable policies over a coding/CI trace.
packs.description: Maps the MISRA Compliance:2020 framework onto ponens policies for MISRA C:2012. The individual coding rules are enforced by static analysis; this pack checks the compliance process around them — analysis before commit, Mandatory never deviated, Required violations covered by approved deviations, re-categorizations recorded in the GRP, and a Guidelines Compliance Summary produced.

packs.name: MISRA C++
packs.summary: The MISRA Compliance:2020 process for MISRA C++:2023 — enforcement, deviations, re-categorization, and compliance summary — as computable policies over a coding/CI trace.
packs.description: Maps the MISRA Compliance:2020 framework onto ponens policies for MISRA C++:2023 (based on C++17). The individual coding rules are enforced by static analysis; this pack checks the compliance process around them — analysis before commit, Mandatory never deviated, Required violations covered by approved deviations, re-categorizations recorded in the GRP, and a Guidelines Compliance Summary produced.

packs.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
packs.summary: The NIST AI RMF 1.0 — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE and the seven trustworthiness characteristics — as computable policies over an AI system's lifecycle record.
packs.description: Maps the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) onto ponens policies. The four core functions become policy groups and the seven trustworthiness characteristics land under MEASURE, making voluntary AI-RMF adoption auditable: risk policies and accountability in place (GOVERN), context/categorization/impacts mapped (MAP), trustworthiness characteristics measured (MEASURE), and risks prioritized/treated with incident response (MANAGE).
packs.source: NIST AI 100-1, AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 (2023).

packs.name: NIST SSDF (Secure Software Development)
packs.summary: The NIST SSDF (SP 800-218) secure-development practices — Prepare the Organization, Protect the Software (integrity/provenance), Produce Well-Secured Software, Respond to Vulnerabilities — as computable policies over a development/release trace.
packs.description: Maps the NIST Secure Software Development Framework onto ponens policies. The four SSDF practice groups become policy groups: PO (security requirements, roles, secure toolchain), PS (release signing, provenance/SBOM, archival), PW (threat modeling, security review/analysis/testing, secure defaults, no open vulnerabilities at release), and RV (ongoing vulnerability analysis, assessment & remediation, root-cause analysis).
